speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 3, Ruth Labovitch, June 13, 1995. So you and Susan were hiding. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, looking for different places where to go sometimes. Not too much forest around there. But just walking and looking for homes to sleep over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you clothed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very lightly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was winter and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wearing some kind of shoes, runners like, maybe like more like closed-in shoe or runner or-- I don't think it was boots. And just there's something on my head, and just I can't even remember whether it was a little coat or just a little jacket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gloves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, I was wearing gloves. I know my sister would lose a glove and then find it again and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you slept in haystacks at night, and then you traveled during the day? Well, when did you go looking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking during the day till we find something. And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, I'd go out at night and leave my sister and come back and find another place. And this was going on for I don't know how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And food? What did you do for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, wherever we stayed, maybe they'd give us a little slice of bread or something. I remember in this one place we stayed, and the lady came and she gave me a small slice of bread with a piece of garlic, and she gave one to my sister. And I was so starved, I finished mine very quickly, and my sister was still eating. And I remember at that point, she still had a little piece-- a piece of bread, like very small piece. And I-- I don't know. She was probably leaving it for later on, left it for later on. But I was hungry, and I sort of took it away from her and I ate it. I ate it, instead of let her finish it later on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And later on, when I thought of it, I would always cry what I did and why I did. But I did it. And we continued looking for more places, or I'd go out at night, or we both walk and find some more places. And then we found this place, and we were hiding in sort of an attic, a little attic. It sort of was a wooden stove, and it was just above the stove but around the corner. And it was maybe, like, two by two. This was very small.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the two of us just staying there, and, you know, the lady told us not to-- not to go out, to stay there. But at night, I've-- I'd go out again and either look for food or other places. I couldn't find. I come back. And again, very desperate. And again, I-- I'm missing my mother so much. And I just felt like, ah, I just can't go on. How much longer am I going to go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: And here I'm in this attic with my sister. And then one morning, I woke up again very early that morning, and I got dressed fast. And I said to myself, I'm leaving my sister. And I wanted to go out before she wakes up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure enough, just as I wanted to walk out, she opens her eyes and she says, you're dressed. Where you going? I says, I'm going away. I'm leaving you here. She says, don't leave me. Don't leave me by myself. I want to go with you. And I says, no, I'm going myself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't even know if I said good-bye to her. And I ran out. And what made me do that? Till today, I don't know. And I left her there by herself, helpless, alone. And she wanted to come with me. And I-- and I just-- just walked out on her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must-- I walked all day that day. It's sort of-- the weather was getting a little nicer, was less cold, was getting nicer. And I just walked and walked and walked, maybe stopping in some forest, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just picking some leaves, whatever, chewing it. And again, at night, I would knock on a door and see if they'd let me in to stay over, to sleep over. And some-- some people would let me. Others would say, no, no, I can't have you. You must be a Jewish kid. Can't have you. Go look other places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you using your own name?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, yes, yes, it was still my own name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as far as I know, my sister with still her name. Course, you know, we weren't asked at that time, like when we were hiding. I-- I don't think they let [INAUDIBLE] our name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take--

speaker: interviewee
text: We still-- no, it--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? --Ukrainian? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: We still had our name, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your own name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My name in Polish was [? Rachela, ?] and Susan's name was [? Zonah. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyways, again, I would find some places to stay and, again, I don't know, two days here, three days there. And if they didn't want me, they said, no, we can't have you. You must go. And I was going from village-- [INAUDIBLE] from village to village to village, walking all day. I don't know how many miles I was making every day, but just walking. The weather was getting nicer now. It's like, you know, maybe more into spring. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then finally I-- I thought, well, I'm going to try the next village. I'm going to try and looking for work. Maybe this way it'll be easier to stay somewhere in one place. And some of them didn't want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, I went to this house on a farm, village, and I tell them I'm a girl from Russia. I'm an orphan. I'm-- I'm an orphan. I'm looking for work. Can you use me? And-- and he said, maybe we can. And the lady, the woman of the house asked me, what's your name? And just without any thinking, I knew I mustn't say [? Rachela, ?] because [? Rachela, ?] it's a Jewish name. And I said, Marusia. And that's a real Russian name. And they said, oh, Marusia. They liked the name right away. And they said, where you from? I said, from Russia. And she sort of took a liking to me right away. And she says to her husband, I think-- I think we can use her, you know? And-- and I stayed there. Where was this? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the village? It was called [NON-ENGLISH], I think, [NON-ENGLISH]. And that's close to a town, Miedzyrzecz, which was-- a lot of Jewish people lived there in that town, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also the Ukraine in this area. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they had two sons. And they wanted me to look after the cattle, the cows, to take them, you know, to the fields and look after them and-- which, I thought, well, of course I'll do it. And then they took away their sons to the Ukraine and to the Ukrainian police, I guess, you know, because they were young Ukrainian guys and they-- and I heard that they were drafted. I don't know. I remember I didn't see them anymore of this house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now I'm looking after all these cattle. And of course, they would serve enough food, and they'd have a lot of-- they'd have bacon and ham. And I knew I mustn't. That's not kosher. I mustn't eat that. And at the table, you know, and they-- and they must know I'm Jewish. If I won't eat that, they will know right away I'm Jewish. And I would put on my plate and pretend I'm eating it and just throw it under the table. So they-- they won't see it, because I would sweep up later on. I would do the housework after. So I knew I'm safe, because I'm going to sweep it. I'd eat every-- the vegetable, whatever, potato. But I was so-- it was so in me that I knew I can't eat no kosher, no bacon or ham, no matter how hungry I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now I'm thinking-- I'm thinking about my sister. What did I do? Where is she? How is she? And I would go on the field. And one day I would go on the field with the cattle, and I-- I was thinking of my sister, and I started to cry so loud. Then I thought, what did I do? Where is she? You know, how am I going to find her now? And I cried very, very loud.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then from the far, I saw a lady. From far away, I saw a lady walking towards me. And as she came near, I start to wipe my eyes. I thought, uh-oh, now she's going to find out I'm a Jewish kid, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she came up to me and she says-- and here I'm-- my eyes are all red, you know-- says to me, why are you crying, little girl? And I says, I'm not crying. She says, yes, but I heard you far away. I heard you cry. Are you not feeling good? I says, no, I'm fine. So why you crying? Again, I says, no, I'm not crying. And my eyes are probably all red, and my hands wet. And I remember her looking at me so pitifully. And she says, OK, you sure you're OK? I says, yes, I'm fine. She said, OK, I'm going to go back. But if you need me, I'm way over there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she walked away, and I thought, well, God is with me again, you know? She didn't take me away or found out I'm Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was going on all the time, you know. And I'd be by myself, at sleep, and just thinking about my sister. What did I do? Why did I do? Where is she? How's she going to survive? And yet, I'm here, and I'm-- I'm staying here, and I do my chores every day. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'd go also-- next door neighbor would be a girl with-- both of us would take the cattle to run on the fields, and both of us would look after them. Not all the time, but many times we were together. So I would-- like another girl. She was a few years older than I am-- than I was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so now I was here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and then they were saying something that the Germans might be running back now. You know, the Russians are-- are advancing, whatever. And they were just burning, like, the highways, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different factories, whatever was-- they were burning everything, whatever they could, running back. And I just remember at night seeing big flames, big fires all over us, you know. And also during this period, a lot of diphtheria broke out on-- on this farm where I was. And three different farmers-- three people died in each-- one person died in each house. And of course, there was no vaccination, nothing. But luckily, it didn't hit our-- this house where I was. So nobody was touched here with diphtheria.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the family treat you, the family that you were staying with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they treated me fine. They treated me--

speaker: interviewer
text: And while you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --as long as I was doing the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear anything about other Jews in the area or other-- or actions against Jews? Was anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was in the-- well, there was very few Jews left already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they'd all been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- in fact, nobody was in the open. If-- if there were any left, they were either hiding or-- you know, they knew that no Jews already-- all the Jews are finished, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] unless they can find them where they were hiding. A lot of them were hiding in forests and in different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I imagine they were still looking. They were still looking for-- and-- and it didn't matter-- children, men, women. If they could find, they-- they found them, and that's-- kill them. Specially towards the end, the Germans just would kill anybody they could find and [? Jewish. ?] What year is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're in '44. We're in '44. And now-- now we're in January, early January, I think, believe, '45? And we wake up in the morning. I-- I guess they-- you know, the people knew that the Russians are-- are advancing and they're coming, and the Germans are going back. But I didn't know. And then one morning, we go out, and we-- or we-- I guess we were woken up. And Russian Red Army unit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came to this village and liberated from the Germans. And this unit-- they were fighting, and they were just resting in this village and this house where I was [INAUDIBLE]. And they were very young boys, maybe 16, 17, 18, from-- from Russia, Uzbekistan, whatever they were from, fighting very hard to-- you know, to win the war. And-- and they were young boys and a young group. And one of the Russian soldiers asked me, what-- what's your name, little girl, or young girl, whatever. And I said, Marusia. And-- and all of them said, oh, Marusia. We know a song about Marusia. And they sang a Russian song to me about Marusia that, till today, I remember the melody, the words of that song. It-- it was, you know, such-- such a relief, whatever, you know, that these Russians are-- are here. And of course, after two, three days, this unit went back to their front lines to fight. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed on the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed on the farm. I continued staying. But after that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I kept asking-- I kept inquiring from other people, is the war going to be over? Are the Germans coming back? Are they coming back? Are they going to come back? And some of them said, no, I don't think so. But they said, the war is still going on. It's not finished. It's not finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, you know, I wouldn't say nothing to these people I stay with. I was inquiring from others. Are the Germans coming back or-- or not? The war going to be finished soon? They said, oh, don't have to worry now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans not coming back. The war will be finished, hopefully soon. So here now one day, I'm in the field with the cattle and this other girl, friend of mine from next neighbor right there. And just then I said to myself, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I'm-- I'm going to leave now. And I asked-- and I ask the girl, I tell her, I says, would you mind taking my cattle back home and tell the people that I'll be back later, later at night, whatever. What was the name of the family that you were staying with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, I wish I could remember. I don't think I even knew their last names. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I always call her [NON-ENGLISH], which means like madam, you know, and [NON-ENGLISH], like sir, you know? I don't think I even called them by their names or-- last name for sure I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you decided to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So right that day I thought, I'm going to run to the city, which is Miedzyrzecz, next to this little-- to this village. And I asked the girl would she mind, you know, looking after the cows and take them home and make sure you tell the people that I went into-- I think I could have told them that I was going to the city, and I'll be back, you know, late at night. And she said, sure, I'll do that. And I walked away, and I walked to the city. And I came to the city, and sure enough, there was some Jewish people already.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were-- they had come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: That came from hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were coming out of hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: From hiding. And there was one family, I stayed with them, not for very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They weren't from my city, because this was a different city. It just whoever was-- you know, like we're sort of-- we'd go to a market and just look for-- see who-- who survived or who we can meet or what Jewish people are there. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then it was amazing because while I was with this-- at this family now in Miedzyrzecz with Jewish people now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one morning I get up, and I'm just covered with a bad rash, you know. And I thought to myself, I bet this lady who I stayed with, she must be cursing me, because what I did, you know? And just that day, the man of the house, of this farm-- how he found me, I was there, I'll never know. He found out where-- where I was, and he came and he talked to me. This was the farmer you'd been staying with.

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmer I was staying with.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he found you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was shocked to see him. And he says, we want you back. We need you. We need you very much. Come back with us. You don't have to be afraid. Or I guess he knew-- he knew then and now he knew, I guess, I was Jewish. We want you. We need you. And we like you. And I says, no, I'm not going back. I'm not going back. And, you know, I guess he was very disappointed in me. And he wanted-- they wanted me back, and I was afraid already to go back there. And I wanted to go on. I was anxious to go, because I knew I have to look for my sister now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyways, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and sure enough after that, they are [INAUDIBLE] something. My rash went away, and I sort of knew that she must have been talking about me. This kind of thing, just sort of we believed in-- in-- in that time. Any signs of something, it was because of this or that or-- And now I'm still not happy staying here in Miedzyrzecz. I want to go to Korzec, the city that was close to my town. And I think this time I was hitchhiking already. I wasn't afraid-- I did-- I wasn't afraid already to hitchhike, you know. I would get some rides to go in to the other city, to Korzec. And I come to Korzec, and again some people-- survivors came out. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody-- I don't think they knew my family, just-- just they were Jewish people, and everybody wants to help everybody. And I stayed with some-- with some lady there. And then one day she tells me, this family want to meet you, because they heard where you were hiding, and they were-- they were hiding under the ground [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the same village. Anyways, I went to meet them, meet this family, and their name was Primac, [? Kayla ?] and [NON-ENGLISH] Primac, and they had a son named Sam. They called him [? Shoma. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were very-- very excited to see me, because on this farm where I was, the next farmer, like I say, they were hiding under the ground, and I would-- would be walking over them, you know? How-- where were they actually hiding? [? You mean ?] under the ground? They had-- was it like a bunker or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a bunker, yes, yes. The farmer made a special bunker for them, like actually under the ground. I guess made enough room for air, whatever, and give them food there, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the farmer fed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And so they were very excited to see me, that, you know, that I was in the same place, except I was, you know, on the outside. And they said to me, well, have you got anywhere to stay? And of course I didn't. This is why you can stay with us in Korzec. And they were wonderful people. They were from Miedzyrzecz themselves, and they were great people. And of course I told them that-- what happened to my sister, that I'm looking. I got to look for my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody came to the house, and they heard who I was, and they knew my family. And one man said to me, you know, he heard that somebody told him that they might have seen my brother-- my second oldest brother, Yankel, that somebody thought they saw him. And I thought, hm, how is that? How can that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but well, I was thinking, and I thought, well, I'll look for him. And I would go to this market where everybody was congregated to-- the survivors, you know, to look for family, friends. And-- and I remember I would just-- I would look at young guys, you know, which weren't too many, of course, left, you know, that age. And I would just go around them and see, maybe it is my brother. But I was hoping against hope. I'd look in their faces. You know, it wasn't him. I knew-- I knew I-- I wouldn't find him. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So now I'm just-- I have to concentrate and look for my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to just pause.
